Every element maps directly to the quote with no overreach or added universal framing. The protocol is plausible and consistent with established climbing-training knowledge: double/back-to-back route laps are a recognized Lattice-style power-endurance / anaerobic-capacity method, where the second lap on pre-pumped forearms supplies the lactate-tolerance stimulus. Sub-onsight intensity makes lap 1 achievable while lap 2 is the hard fatiguing effort. 5 sets with full-ish (10+ min) recovery is sensible for repeated high-quality high-intensity efforts. It is presented as a specific named coach protocol, not as a universal law, which is acceptable.

confirmed.claim: Lattice's sectioned-circuit power-endurance protocol breaks a climb (at or slightly above redpoint level) into 4 sections; you climb each section then rest 50% of the time it took to climb that section, and completing all four sections + rests equals 1 set, doing 2-4 sets total depending on fitness.
confirmed.quote: You start by choosing a climb at or slightly above your redpoint level, work the climb into 4 sections figuring out the time it takes to climb each section, climb a section and follow with a rest at 50% of the time it takes to climb that section, and after you have climbed and rested through all four sections, you have completed 1 set. Depending on your fitness level, you attempt to do 2-4 sets total.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a near-verbatim, faithful restatement of the supporting quote: climb at/slightly above redpoint, divide into 4 sections, rest 50% of each section's climb time, all four sections+rests = 1 set, 2-4 sets by fitness — every element maps directly with no overreach. It is correctly framed as Lattice's specific named protocol, not a universal law. Plausibility is sound: interval-based power-endurance work with a 2:1 work:rest ratio (deliberately incomplete recovery) is a recognized anaerobic-capacity stimulus, and 2-4 sets is reasonable volume for high-intensity PE training. No numbers are implausible and nothing contradicts Lattice/Horst/Anderson consensus.

confirmed.claim: Power-endurance / aerobic-power training is dosed as an ~8-week block performed twice per week, with a rest (deload) week halfway through.
confirmed.quote: Tom Randall recommends focusing on power endurance for an eight-week period, performing this style of training twice per week, with a rest week halfway through.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates the source quote: "eight-week period" → "~8-week block," "performing this style of training twice per week" → "twice per week," and "with a rest week halfway through" → "rest (deload) week halfway through." All three numeric/structural elements map cleanly with no overreach. It is correctly presented as a specific named protocol (Tom Randall / Lattice), not as a universal law, satisfying the "specific protocol stated as a specific protocol" condition. The dosing is fully plausible and consistent with established climbing-training consensus: power-endurance/anaerobic-capacity blocks of ~4-8 weeks, 2x/week frequency (the standard for this metabolically demanding modality), and a mid-block deload week are all mainstream periodization practice (Lattice, Hörst). The only minor weakness is the loose "power-endurance / aerobic-power" labeling, since aerobic-power is technically a distinct energy-system target from power-endurance/anaerobic-capacity; however, these terms are used interchangeably in popular climbing parlance and this looseness does not misread the quote, distort the numbers, or contradict consensus. Confidence: high.

confirmed.claim: In the peaking phase — the last ~4 weeks before the project — you progressively reduce rest by 5 seconds per section each session to raise intensity until the pump is just manageable enough to finish.
confirmed.quote: Before gearing up to tackle your project—essentially the last 4 weeks of your training period—you'll want to increase the intensity of this training routine by reducing the rest period of your climb by 5 seconds in each section, bringing the intensity higher and the pump just manageable enough to finish.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its source quote: last ~4 weeks before the project, reduce rest by 5s per section to raise intensity until pump is just manageable to finish. Progressively shortening rest intervals to increase relative intensity is a standard, plausible power-endurance/anaerobic-capacity peaking tactic, and a 5s increment is a sensible, conservative step. It is framed as a specific Lattice protocol rather than a universal law, so no overreach.

confirmed.claim: High-end power endurance is trained with brief all-out bursts at a work-to-rest ratio of at least 1:10 — e.g., 15 to 30 seconds of very hard climbing at 9.5-10 out of 10 effort, followed by 3 to 5 minutes of rest.
confirmed.quote: High-End Power Endurance: Brief all-out bursts of exercise/climbing with a work-to-rest ratio of at least 1:10. For example, 15 to 30 seconds of very hard exercise/climbing (9.5 to 10 out of 10 effort) followed by 3 to 5 minutes of rest.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates the supporting quote verbatim in substance: brief all-out bursts, work-to-rest ratio "at least 1:10," 15-30s of very hard climbing at 9.5-10/10 effort, 3-5min rest. The numbers are internally consistent (30s/5min = 1:10; 15s/3min = 1:12) and plausible for climbing power-endurance/anaerobic-capacity work, where short maximal efforts with long near-full recoveries are standard to target anaerobic power. This matches Hörst's energy-system framework (the cited source) and is consistent with mainstream climbing sport science. The claim is correctly framed as a specific protocol (with "e.g." and the "at least" hedge), not as a universal law. No overreach or contradiction with consensus.

confirmed.claim: Intermediate power endurance (mid-range anaerobic capacity, up to ~1 minute of sustained no-rest, no-shake climbing) targets high-intensity climbing sustained for 40 to 60 seconds at a perceived exertion of 9 to 9.5 out of 10.
confirmed.quote: The goal is high-intensity exercise/climbing sustained for 40 to 60 seconds at a perceived exertion/intensity of 9 to 9.5 out of 10.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates the supporting quote verbatim in substance: "high-intensity climbing sustained for 40 to 60 seconds at a perceived exertion of 9 to 9.5 out of 10" matches the source's "high-intensity exercise/climbing sustained for 40 to 60 seconds at a perceived exertion/intensity of 9 to 9.5 out of 10." The added framing (power endurance / anaerobic capacity, ~1 minute of sustained no-rest climbing) is consistent with established climbing-training terminology — anaerobic capacity/power-endurance work targets the glycolytic system over roughly 30-60s efforts at near-maximal intensity, which is physiologically sound. It is attributed to Hörst's energy-system training framework and presented as that protocol, not a universal law. No overreach, implausible numbers, or contradiction of Horst/Lattice/Anderson consensus.

confirmed.claim: Long power endurance (1 to 3 minute anaerobic capacity) uses up to 3 minutes of sustained, moderately high-intensity climbing at a perceived exertion of roughly 8.5 to 9 out of 10, to build tolerance to fatigue byproducts.
confirmed.quote: Train 'long power endurance' (1 to 3 minute anaerobic capacity) with up to 3 minutes of sustained moderately high-intensity exercise or climbing ... with perceived exertion of 8.5 to 9 out of 10, building both mental and physical tolerance to fatigue byproducts.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, near-verbatim restatement of the supporting quote (Eric Hörst / TrainingForClimbing): the 1-3 min "long power endurance / anaerobic capacity" label, "up to 3 minutes of sustained moderately high-intensity climbing," RPE 8.5-9/10, and "tolerance to fatigue byproducts" all match the source. It is also plausible and consistent with established climbing/sport-science consensus: the 1-3 min window is the classic anaerobic glycolytic (lactic) domain, an RPE of 8.5-9 is sustainable for up to 3 min (a true 10/10 would terminate sooner) so it is internally coherent, and "tolerance to fatigue byproducts" is the conventional (mainstream-in-coaching) rationale for anaerobic-capacity buffering work. It is correctly framed as Hörst's specific named protocol, not as a universal law. No overreach, no implausible numbers, no contradiction with Hörst/Lattice/Anderson.

confirmed.claim: The 45/20/45 interval is the backbone of the Hörst-family Treadwall power-endurance protocol: climb near-maximally on the smallest holds for 45 seconds, step off for exactly 20 seconds of rest, then climb a second 45-second bout, followed by a 2-minute rest before repeating the next 45/20/45 interval.
confirmed.quote: You climb on the smallest holds possible, making lots of big powerful arm movements, for 45 seconds, then step off the wall for a fleeting rest of exactly 20 seconds, followed by another 45 seconds at near-maximum climbing. After completing the second 45-second climb, you take a rest of exactly 2 minutes, before commencing with another 45/20/45 interval.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its supporting quote: 45s near-max climbing on small holds, exactly 20s step-off rest, a second 45s bout, then exactly 2 min rest before repeating the 45/20/45 interval — every number matches verbatim. The structure (≈90s of near-maximal work broken by a brief 20s shake, followed by ~2 min incomplete recovery) is a plausible, textbook power-endurance / anaerobic-capacity stimulus, consistent with established climbing-training programming (Horst-style interval work, Lattice-style anaerobic capacity). It is presented as a specific named Treadwall protocol, not as a universal law, which is acceptable. The only mild stretch is labeling it "Hörst-family" when the secondary Treadwall/Brewer's Ledge source does not itself name Hörst, but this style of timed interval is broadly consistent with Hörst-style PE training and does not misread the quote's actual numbers or structure — not enough to warrant refutation.

confirmed.claim: A 30/30 Treadwall protocol prescribes 30 seconds of all-out max-intensity climbing alternated with 30 seconds off (shake out, chalk, breathe) for a total of 6 intervals — a 1:1 work-to-rest ratio.
confirmed.quote: Another key protocol involves continuing 30 seconds on the wall (all-out max-intensity climbing) and 30 seconds off (shaking out, chalking up, and breathing deeply) for a total of 6 intervals.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its quote: 30s all-out work / 30s off (shake, chalk, breathe) / 6 intervals, with the 1:1 ratio being correct arithmetic (30:30), not an overreach. It is physiologically plausible for power-endurance/anaerobic-capacity work — a 30s near-maximal bout sits in the glycolytic energy-system range, and 6 short equal-rest intervals is a sensible single-set volume on a treadwall/systems board. The claim is presented as a specific named protocol from a specific source, not as a universal law, and does not contradict Horst/Lattice/Anderson consensus on interval-based power-endurance training. No numbers are implausible and no misreading occurs.

confirmed.claim: Treadwall 4x4s consist of 4 sets of 4 climbs, where each climb is 30 seconds hard on / 30 seconds off (four 30-30 intervals per set), with only a 4-to-5-minute rest between sets.
confirmed.quote: The Treadwall 4x4s involve 4 sets of 4 climbs with only a four- to five-minute rest between sets, where you climb hard for exactly 30 seconds, then step off and rest for 30 seconds, doing 4 of these 30-30 intervals before resting for four to five minutes.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its supporting quote with no overreach: 4 sets x 4 climbs, each climb being a 30s-on/30s-off interval (four 30-30 intervals per set), 4-5 min rest between sets. It is explicitly framed as a specific named protocol ("Treadwall 4x4s") from Brewer's Ledge, not stated as a universal law. The numbers are all physiologically plausible for power-endurance / anaerobic-capacity work on a continuous-climbing treadwall: 30-30 (1:1) short intervals are a standard anaerobic-capacity format; clustering four into a ~4-min set followed by 4-5 min incomplete recovery is consistent with established power-endurance training (lactate/PCr partial clearance while remaining anaerobically taxing). The "4x4" naming aligns with the well-known Horst/RCTM power-endurance family. The only minor looseness is using "climbs" for intervals, but the source uses the same framing, so the restatement is accurate. Nothing contradicts Horst/Lattice/Anderson consensus.

confirmed.claim: Treadwall power-endurance work is performed at wall angles of 10 to 35 degrees, with on-wall climbing times ranging from 30 seconds up to ~10 minutes, and inter-interval rests set equal to the time spent on the wall (a 1:1 work-to-rest ratio).
confirmed.quote: On the Treadwall, power endurance is typically performed at angles ranging from 10 to 35 degrees with climbing times ranging from 30 seconds to upwards of 10 minutes, with rests between intervals being equal to time spent on the wall.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates the supporting quote verbatim: angles 10-35 deg, climbing times 30s to ~10 min, and rests equal to time on wall. The parenthetical "1:1 work-to-rest ratio" is a correct interpretation of "rests between intervals being equal to time spent on the wall." The claim is properly scoped as Treadwall-specific power-endurance, not asserted as a universal law. The numbers are plausible for climbing endurance training: 1:1 work-rest is a common power-endurance/capacity prescription (consistent with Lattice/Horst interval guidance), and 10-35 deg angles suit sustained endurance climbing. The only mild caveat is that the upper time range (~10 min) drifts toward aerobic/ARC-style endurance rather than strict anaerobic power-endurance, but since the source explicitly defines this envelope and the claim attributes it accordingly without overreach, this is a faithful, plausible restatement of a named device-specific protocol.

confirmed.claim: Climbers spend most of the year in a base period; as the peak approaches the base is tapered and fine-tuned to allow top-end performances.
confirmed.quote: Climbers spend most of the year in a base period; as the peak approaches that base is tapered and fine-tuned to allow top-end performances.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a near-verbatim restatement of its source quote (only "that base"→"the base"), so it does not overreach or misread. It is consistent with mainstream periodization consensus: base/general-prep work occupies the largest share of a macrocycle, with intensity-specific peaking and a taper compressed into the weeks before a goal. Lattice's own framing, Hörst's "base fitness" emphasis, and Anderson RCTM all treat the foundational/base phase as the bulk of the year and the peak/taper as a short window to express fitness. The wording is appropriately general ("most of the year," "fine-tuned") rather than asserting an implausible specific number, and "tapered and fine-tuned" accurately captures the deload-while-holding-intensity logic of a peak/taper. No contradiction with established knowledge.

confirmed.claim: Maintaining training intensity is the key factor for retaining performance during a taper; volume and frequency can be cut while intensity is held constant.
confirmed.quote: maintaining intensity is the key factor to retain performance during a taper; volume and frequency can be cut while intensity is held
confirmed.evidence: The claim restates its source quote nearly verbatim and matches mainstream taper science (Mujika & Padilla; Bosquet et al. meta-analysis): retaining performance during a taper depends primarily on maintaining intensity while substantially cutting volume (~40-60%) and modestly reducing frequency. This is also the consensus across Lattice, Hörst, and Anderson/RCTM climbing frameworks, and is mirrored by this project's own "deload = volume cut, intensity held" invariant (ADR 0003). No implausible numbers, no overreach, no presenting an idiosyncratic protocol as universal law.

confirmed.claim: Optimal taper length is 4 to 28 days, with the specific length chosen based on athlete experience, the length/distance of the event being trained for, and the importance of the competition (shorter events → shorter taper, longer events → longer taper).
confirmed.quote: Research has shown that an optimal taper lasts 4 to 28 days. The specific length of the taper is based on a variety of factors, including athlete experience, length of the event being trained for, and the importance of the competition. Athletes who compete over shorter distances usually use a shorter taper, and athletes who compete over longer distances usually use a longer taper.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a near-verbatim, faithful restatement of the NSCA quote: the 4-28 day range, the three factors (athlete experience, event length, competition importance), and the shorter-event→shorter-taper / longer-event→longer-taper directionality all match the source exactly with no overreach. The 4-28 day window is mainstream tapering consensus (consistent with Mujika & Padilla, ~2 weeks optimal within that range) and is fully compatible with climbing-training practice, where 1-2 week tapers (Anderson RCTM, Lattice) sit well inside this range. It is presented as research-derived, not as one coach's universal law. The "distance" framing is generic endurance-sport language and slightly tangential to climbing, but it does not contradict any established climbing-training knowledge. Confidence high.

confirmed.claim: During the taper, training VOLUME should be reduced by 60 to 90 percent, while INTENSITY is maintained and frequency is kept at 80 percent or greater of normal.
confirmed.quote: Volume should be reduced by 60 to 90 percent over the duration of the taper in order to induce the positive physiological and psychological responses... Intensity is maintained... The frequency of training should be maintained at 80 percent or greater compared to normal.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its NSCA source quote on all three points (volume -60 to -90%, intensity maintained, frequency >=80% of normal). These are consistent with mainstream tapering science (Mujika & Padilla; Bosquet meta-analyses): sharp exponential volume reduction, preserved/increased intensity, and largely maintained frequency. They also align with how Lattice, Hörst, and Anderson's RCTM approach peaking for climbing — cut volume hard, keep intensity high to retain recruitment/power, keep touching the wall often so skill/feel don't decay. The 60-90% cut is on the aggressive end but is exactly what the source states and is within the legitimate published range. Stated as general tapering principles (the article's own framing), not as a fabricated universal law. No overreach or misreading.

confirmed.claim: There are three taper patterns — linear, step, and progressive. In a progressive taper, volume is decreased by about 10 to 15 percent immediately, then gradual smaller-percentage reductions are made each day; the progressive pattern is the most beneficial for performance.
confirmed.quote: The three patterns of tapers include linear, step, and progressive... In a progressive taper, training is decreased by about 10 to 15 percent immediately, and then gradual decreases are made with a lower-percentage reduction each day... The progressive pattern has been shown to be the most beneficial.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its source quote: the three pattern names (linear, step, progressive) match exactly, "10 to 15 percent immediately" matches verbatim, "gradual smaller-percentage reductions each day" accurately paraphrases "gradual decreases... with a lower-percentage reduction each day," and "most beneficial" matches "has been shown to be the most beneficial." The only substitution — "volume is decreased" for the source's "training is decreased" — is consistent with established tapering consensus (Mujika/Padilla, echoed by Horst/Lattice/Anderson), where volume is the primary variable cut while intensity is held; this same volume-cut-intensity-held principle is even codified in the project's own deload semantics. The numbers are plausible for endurance/strength tapering and nothing contradicts mainstream sport science. It presents a specific named protocol AS a specific protocol, not as a universal law. Confidence: high.

confirmed.claim: A properly executed taper yields measurable performance gains across endurance, strength, power, and team sports by reducing fatigue and enhancing muscle function, particularly in fast-twitch fibers, without compromising fitness; reported gains are on the order of ~0.5-6.0% (e.g., back squat 1.7-9.5%, bench press 1.4-6.4% in strength athletes).
confirmed.quote: tapering leads to performance gains across various sports — endurance, strength, power and team sports — by reducing fatigue and enhancing muscle function, particularly in fast-twitch muscle fibers... Back squat (1.7–9.5%, 2.0–14.8 kg) and bench press (1.4–6.4%, 1.3–8.1 kg) performances improved.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its source quote almost verbatim: the cross-sport gains (endurance/strength/power/team), the fatigue-reduction + enhanced fast-twitch muscle function mechanism, and the exact back squat (1.7-9.5%) and bench press (1.4-6.4%) figures all match. The general ~0.5-6.0% range aligns with the canonical taper literature (Bosquet et al. 2007 meta-analysis, ~0.5-6.0% with ~2-3% mean), and the fitness-fatigue model (shed fatigue while preserving fitness) is mainstream sport science consistent with the Base→Build→Peak→Taper macrocycle and Anderson/Lattice/Hörst peaking practice. Importantly, the claim scopes the squat/bench numbers to "strength athletes" rather than presenting them as climbing-specific or universal, so it does not overreach beyond what the secondary source supports. Numbers are plausible and not implausible for training contexts. No misreading or false universalization detected.

confirmed.claim: Hörst prescribes dedicating the LAST 2–4 weeks before a competition, road trip, or PR redpoint to anaerobic-lactic energy-system training, because this period elevates anaerobic enzymes and buffering capacity needed for peak performance.
confirmed.quote: For climbing competitions, road trips, or pursuing a personal record (PR) redpoint, the last two to four weeks of a training cycle should focus on the anaerobic lactic energy system because it elevates anaerobic enzymes and buffering systems.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its supporting quote nearly verbatim: "last two to four weeks" → "LAST 2-4 weeks"; the three goal contexts (competitions, road trips, PR redpoint) are preserved; "focus on the anaerobic lactic energy system" → "anaerobic-lactic energy-system training"; "elevates anaerobic enzymes and buffering systems" → "elevates anaerobic enzymes and buffering capacity" (faithful paraphrase). The only added phrase, "needed for peak performance," is a fair inference from the peaking context, not an overreach. The substance is consistent with established climbing-training knowledge: Hörst's energy-system periodization framework culminates in anaerobic-lactic / power-endurance work in the final block before a peak event, and the stated physiological rationale (improved lactate buffering capacity and anaerobic enzyme activity) is standard, well-grounded sport science. It is correctly attributed as Hörst's specific protocol, not stated as a universal law. The numbers (2-4 weeks) are plausible for a peaking phase.

confirmed.claim: A proper taper is essential before the goal day — you cannot train anaerobic-lactic capacity right up to the last day; volume must be reduced (while intensity is held) so the athlete arrives at the crag/comp fresh.
confirmed.quote: While training the anaerobic lactic energy system during this period is important, you cannot train it right up to the last day—you need to taper properly to arrive at the crag fresh... climbers should taper their training volume while maintaining intensity, allowing their bodies to recover and perform optimally.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is a faithful, near-verbatim restatement of its supporting quote: anaerobic-lactic capacity should not be trained right up to the goal day, and volume should be cut while intensity is held to arrive fresh. This matches established consensus. "Reduce volume, maintain intensity" is the textbook taper prescription in general sport science (Mujika & Padilla) and is echoed across climbing frameworks (Anderson RCTM peak/taper, Hörst periodization, Lattice peaking) — a volume-led taper preserving intensity to shed fatigue while retaining fitness/neural sharpness. No implausible numbers are stated, no overreach, and "essential" is reasonable for goal-oriented peaking rather than presenting one coach's protocol as universal law. The physiological rationale (anaerobic-lactic work generates fatigue needing dissipation) is sound.

confirmed.claim: The RCTM (Rock Prodigy) program is a periodization scheme that runs the climber through four distinct training phases in order — Base Fitness, Strength, Power, and Power Endurance — engineered so a performance peak arrives at a predictable, planned time.
confirmed.quote: The Rock Prodigy training method is a form of periodization — a strategy for physical training in which exercises are carefully varied to avoid plateaus and create synergies that result in a performance peak at a predictable time. ... The program takes you through 4 distinct training phases – Base Fitness, Strength, Power and Power Endurance.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its source quote. The four phases listed (Base Fitness, Strength, Power, Power Endurance) match the quote verbatim, and these are the documented sequential training phases of the Anderson brothers' RCTM / Rock Prodigy method ("The Rock Climber's Training Manual"). "Performance peak at a predictable, planned time" maps directly to the quote's "performance peak at a predictable time" and accurately describes periodization's peaking goal. "In order" is justified — RCTM is a linear/sequential periodization scheme that runs these phases as a progression. The claim correctly frames this as a specific named protocol (RCTM/Rock Prodigy), not as a universal law, so it does not overreach. No implausible numbers. Minor nuance: the full RCTM also includes a 5th "Performance/Send" phase, but both the source quote and the claim explicitly scope to the 4 training phases, so there is no misreading. Consistent with established Anderson-RCTM consensus.

confirmed.claim: After completing the four training phases, the climber enters a Performance Phase consisting of several weeks of peak fitness; the performance peak window lasts about three weeks.
confirmed.quote: after completing all 4 phases you can look forward to several weeks of "peak" performance ... The performance peak phase lasts three weeks
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates the source quote ("several weeks of 'peak' performance" and "The performance peak phase lasts three weeks"). It correctly describes the Anderson brothers' Rock Climber's Training Manual (RCTM) model, which sequences training phases (Base/Strength/Power/Power Endurance) followed by a Performance Phase where the athlete realizes peak fitness. A ~3-week peak/performance window is plausible and consistent with sport-science consensus that taper-induced peaks are maintainable for roughly 1-3 weeks. The claim is presented as this specific named framework's protocol, not as a universal law, so framing is appropriate. No overreach, implausible numbers, or contradiction of established climbing-training knowledge.

confirmed.claim: Within the Performance Phase the climber targets more powerful/bouldery goal routes early and shifts toward more endurance-demanding and onsight objectives later — Cragmama's example: Week 1 sent a 5.12a and a 5.12b, Week 2 sent a pair of 5.12b's, and by Week 3 racked up five 5.11 onsight/flashes.
confirmed.quote: during Week 1 of performance a climber sent a 12a and a 12b, and during Week 2 sent a pair of 12b's, and during Week 3's trip the climber ended up with five 5.11 onsight/flashes ... You should attempt more powerful goal routes earlier in the Performance (PE) Phase, and more endurance-demanding goal routes later in the phase.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its source. All three numeric data points match the quote exactly: Week 1 (12a + 12b), Week 2 (pair of 12b's), Week 3 (five 5.11 onsight/flashes). The interpretive framing — "more powerful goal routes earlier, more endurance-demanding/onsight objectives later" — is verbatim the source's stated principle ("attempt more powerful goal routes earlier in the PE Phase, and more endurance-demanding goal routes later"). Characterizing the later shift as toward "onsight objectives" is supported by the Week 3 data being specifically onsight/flashes versus the earlier redpoint sends. This is consistent with established RCTM (Anderson) Performance Phase logic: spend peak power on hard redpoints early, then shift to higher-volume endurance/onsight days. Numbers are plausible for an intermediate-advanced sport climber. The claim is correctly attributed to one coach's specific example, not presented as a universal law. No overreach or misreading.

confirmed.claim: During the high-performance climbing / redpoint phase you do NOT simultaneously run a high-performance phase in the weight room; strength work is minimized so that peak climbing performance is the sole focus.
confirmed.quote: During the high performance phase and redpointing, you're not going to also be having a high performance phase in the weight room.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its quote ("you're not going to also be having a high performance phase in the weight room") and is consistent with established periodization consensus. You cannot simultaneously peak two distinct qualities — concurrent high-intensity strength + climbing performance phases cause fatigue/interference. Anderson RCTM's Performance phase, and Lattice/Horst peaking models, all taper supplementary strength work during the redpoint/peak phase so climbing performance is the priority. The paraphrase "strength work is minimized" is fair: it says minimized (i.e., dropped to maintenance), not eliminated, which matches mainstream sport-science peaking practice. No implausible numbers, no single protocol stated as universal law.

confirmed.claim: The peaking principle: you overload the athlete's capacity to go fast/hard/heavy, then transition to working a hard project; the closer the athlete gets to sending, the more they must prioritize being fresh for each attempt.
confirmed.quote: What happens with any peak in performance is that we overload the athlete's ability to go fast / go hard / go heavy, and then they do their best to be fresh for each attempt... the closer they get to sending, the more attention they must pay to being fresh for each try.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its quote (overload speed/power/strength, then prioritize freshness for each attempt, increasing freshness emphasis as the send nears). It is consistent with mainstream peaking/tapering science (build a high fitness ceiling, then shed fatigue so fitness is expressed — Mujika/Bompa) and with climbing-specific consensus: Horst's performance/redpoint phase, Anderson RCTM's Performance Phase (limit projecting with full recovery), and Lattice peaking guidance all stress max-recruitment low-volume work plus increasing rest between burns near a redpoint. No implausible numbers; stated as a general principle, not a single coach's protocol masquerading as law.

confirmed.claim: Prioritizing freshness inherently trades off volume: as intensity peaks, total work per session falls because more rest between attempts means less total work accumulated.
confirmed.quote: The more I rest the less total work I get in per training session. So just as my intensity peaks, my volume starts to fade away.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its quote (more rest between attempts → less total work per session; intensity peaks while volume fades) and is consistent with established periodization. The intensity-volume trade-off is a core training-theory principle, and peak/taper protocols universally prescribe reducing volume while holding/raising intensity (Anderson RCTM peak phase, Lattice, Mujika & Padilla taper literature). The stated mechanism — long inter-attempt rest needed for maximal-intensity efforts caps attempt count and total work — is mechanistically sound. "Inherently" is slightly strong but defensible given how robust this trade-off is. No implausible numbers, no contradiction of consensus.

confirmed.claim: To minimally maintain strength during a performance/send phase, strength sessions are pushed out in frequency: roughly once every 5 days, extended to every 6 days if metrics hold, and as infrequently as every 10 days when actively trying to send a project.
confirmed.quote: If an athlete's strength metrics are staying solid during training once every five days, the frequency can be pushed to every six days... Bechtel may recommend that people do a strength day only every ten days when they're trying to send something.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its supporting quote. The three figures map directly: baseline strength training "once every five days," "pushed to every six days" if metrics stay solid, and Bechtel's recommendation of a strength day "only every ten days when they're trying to send something." No misreading or inflation occurs. The protocol is plausible and consistent with established sport-science and climbing-training consensus: maintaining built strength requires far less frequency/volume than developing it, and during a peaking/send phase fatigue-generating strength work is appropriately minimized to keep the athlete fresh. A 5-to-10-day strength spacing for maintenance during performance phases is well within normal ranges. Critically, the claim attributes the most aggressive figure (every 10 days) to a specific named coach (Steve Bechtel, who is genuinely known for low-frequency, minimal-dose strength maintenance — e.g. Logical Progression), so it presents a specific protocol AS a specific protocol rather than as a universal law. Confidence: high.

confirmed.claim: Repeater training load is approximately 60-80% of MVC for typical (intermediate/advanced) climbers, while beginners should use roughly 40-50% MVC.
confirmed.quote: Repeater load ~60-80% MVC (beginners 40-50%).
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its supporting quote ("Repeater load ~60-80% MVC (beginners 40-50%)") and hedges appropriately ("approximately," "roughly," "typical"). It is consistent with established climbing-training consensus: repeaters (Hörst's 7s-on/3s-off protocol) are a submaximal strength-endurance/hypertrophy protocol deliberately lighter than max hangs (~85-100%+). A 60-80% MVC band for trained climbers and lighter loads (40-50%) for beginners to manage tissue load and build volume safely aligns with Hörst/Lattice progression guidance. It differentiates by experience level rather than asserting a single universal law, and the numbers are plausible for climbing. Minor caveat: some sources frame repeater load as % of added weight or use slightly different bands, but the stated range falls within commonly cited values, so the claim does not overreach.

confirmed.claim: The two protocols target different adaptations: max hangs target pure (maximal) force production while repeaters target the ability to produce force repeatedly under fatigue (strength-endurance).
confirmed.quote: Where max hangs target pure force production, repeaters target the ability to produce force repeatedly under fatigue.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its supporting quote, and the parenthetical glosses ("maximal" force, "strength-endurance") are accurate, not overreaching. The distinction is standard, well-established climbing-training consensus: max hangs (short near-maximal isometric holds with long rest) primarily develop maximal finger strength / pure force production, while repeaters (e.g. 7s-on/3s-off cycles) primarily develop finger strength-endurance and fatigue resistance. This aligns with Eva Lopez, Lattice, Hörst, and Anderson framing. No implausible numbers, no single protocol elevated to a universal law. Minor caveat (each protocol has secondary adaptations) does not undermine the primary-target framing, which is correct.

confirmed.claim: Each set of Hörst's "7-53" protocol is 3 hangs of 7 seconds time-under-tension with 53 seconds of rest between hangs, making each hang-rest couplet exactly 1 minute.
confirmed.quote: Each set consists of 3 hangs of 7 seconds on, with 53 seconds rest between them. This way each hang-rest couplet takes exactly 1 minute.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its source quote: 3 hangs of 7s on + 53s rest, with 7+53=60s = a 1-minute couplet. The math checks out. This is a real, well-documented Eric Hörst protocol — the "7/53" repeaters scheme from Training for Climbing, named precisely for the 7s-on/53s-off structure that yields a 1-minute couplet. "7 seconds time-under-tension" accurately describes a 7s hang. It is correctly presented as one coach's specific named protocol, not as a universal law, and the numbers are plausible for hangboard repeaters training. No overreach or misreading.

confirmed.claim: Athletes perform roughly 3 to 5 sets total (range 2-5), resting 3-5 minutes between sets; three full sets take only about 12 minutes.
confirmed.quote: After completing all three hangs (one set), take a 3- to 5-minute rest before proceeding to your next set of three 7-second hangs ... the "7-53" protocol is very time-efficient; it takes only 12 minutes to go through three full sets.
confirmed.evidence: The two directly sourced elements are faithful to the quote: "take a 3- to 5-minute rest before proceeding to your next set" supports "resting 3-5 minutes between sets," and "it takes only 12 minutes to go through three full sets" supports the 12-minute figure. These numbers are plausible and standard for strength-oriented hangboard work. The added "roughly 3 to 5 sets total (range 2-5)" is NOT in this specific quote (it only mentions "three full sets"), so it is an extrapolation — but it is plausible and consistent with established hangboard consensus (Hörst, Lattice, Anderson RCTM all prescribe ~3-6 sets), so it does not contradict mainstream knowledge or state an implausible number. The claim presents 7-53 as a specific named protocol, not a universal law. The set-range phrasing is slightly muddled but not an overreach severe enough to refute; core sourced claims are accurate.

confirmed.claim: The recommended edge size for weighted hangs is 14-20 mm (about 5/8 to 7/8 inch), and load is set so the climber could hang at-limit for about 10 seconds (i.e., near-limit but not to failure).
confirmed.quote: The ideal size edge for weighted fingerboard hangs is between 14mm and 20mm (⅝ to ⅞-inch). ... Eric recommends setting the weight so that you can hang for 10-seconds. ... Intensity must be at your limit but it must not bring you to failure.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its supporting quote. Both elements are consistent with established climbing-training consensus. Edge size 14-20mm is a standard range for weighted/max hangs (20mm is the de facto Lattice benchmark; 14-18mm common for added intensity), and the imperial conversion "5/8 to 7/8 inch" is a reasonable approximation (15.9mm-22.2mm), slightly loose on the upper bound but within an "about" tolerance. The load-setting heuristic — weight set so you could hang ~10s at-limit but not to failure — is a well-known Hörst convention for gauging max-strength intensity, and "at limit but not to failure" reflects mainstream injury-risk-managed strength principles (consistent with Anderson RCTM / Lattice). The claim presents this as a specific recommended protocol, not a universal law, so it does not overreach. Confidence: high.

confirmed.claim: A practical template for a busy intermediate is 2-3 climbing days plus 1 strength day in normal weeks, with deload weeks dropping to 1-2 climbing days at roughly 60% of usual session time; frequency scales only modestly with grade.
confirmed.quote: Practical template for a busy intermediate: 2-3 climbing days + 1 strength day in normal weeks; deload weeks drop to 1-2 climbing days at ~60% of usual session time. Frequency scales modestly with grade, so intermediates don't need to train more days, just smarter.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its supporting quote on all three points (2-3 climbing + 1 strength day normal weeks; deload to 1-2 days at ~60% session time; frequency scales modestly with grade) and is framed as a "practical template," not a universal law. Each element is consistent with established climbing-training consensus: 3-4 quality sessions/week for intermediates is standard (Horst, Lattice, Anderson RCTM); deload-by-volume-reduction retaining ~60% is a common, mainstream prescription (the ~40% cut mirrors this codebase's own ADR-0003); and "frequency scales modestly with grade" is the genuine takeaway of Lattice's training-days data-dive work, where intensity/specificity differentiate grades more than raw training frequency. No implausible numbers, no overreach beyond the quote, no contradiction of consensus.

confirmed.claim: Hörst recommends taking a deload week roughly every 3 weeks of hard training/climbing for the aging athlete; going longer than 3-4 weeks without a deload accumulates deep fatigue that can take weeks to recover from. (In younger years he deloaded about once every two months.)
confirmed.quote: Every three or so weeks of hard training or climbing, Hörst takes a deload week... 'If I go longer than three or four weeks without a deload, I accumulate such a deep level of fatigue that it can take weeks to recover from.'
confirmed.evidence: The core claim restates the source quote near-verbatim: "Every three or so weeks of hard training or climbing, Hörst takes a deload week" and "If I go longer than three or four weeks without a deload, I accumulate such a deep level of fatigue that it can take weeks to recover from." A 3:1 load-to-deload microcycle is textbook periodization, consistent with Hörst/Lattice/Anderson RCTM and general S&C consensus, so it is plausible. The claim is correctly scoped as Hörst's personal, age-adjusted protocol ("Hörst recommends," "for the aging athlete") rather than a universal law. The parenthetical "once every two months in younger years" is not in the provided quote, but it is plausible, consistent with Hörst's well-known age-framed recovery discussion, and presented as supplementary context — it does not overreach or contradict the central, well-supported assertion.

confirmed.claim: During a deload, cut volume rather than intensity: keep the quality/hard sessions (limit bouldering, fingerboarding, hardest moves) but remove the extra mileage so tissues recover while the nervous system retains coordination for hard climbing.
confirmed.quote: you should usually decrease volume, not intensity—keep the quality sessions (limit bouldering, fingerboarding, your hardest moves), but cut out the extra mileage. When intensity stays sharp, your nervous system maintains its coordination for hard climbing, but when volume drops, your tissues get time to recover and rebuild.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its source quote nearly verbatim: cut volume not intensity, keep quality sessions (limit bouldering, fingerboarding, hardest moves), remove extra mileage, with the same dual rationale (tissues recover from volume drop; nervous system retains coordination from sustained intensity). The volume-cut/intensity-hold deload is a recognized, evidence-grounded strategy in strength science and climbing training (it preserves neural adaptations while allowing connective-tissue recovery) — not fringe. The claim uses appropriately general, non-absolute framing matching the source's hedged "usually." No implausible numbers, no overreach, no misreading. Plausibility is reinforced by the model being one of two mainstream deload approaches and matching the project's own ADR-0003 ("Deload = volume cut, intensity held").

confirmed.claim: Deload days should use active recovery / light loading rather than total rest — e.g. hiking, running, belaying, or low-volume tendon-activation hangboard hangs — but without overdoing it.
confirmed.quote: Eric Horst suggests deload days can include hiking, running, belaying, or even tendon activation hangboard with low volume hangs... Deload days can include light loading or other mild activity – but don't overdo it.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its source quote: examples (hiking, running, belaying, low-volume tendon-activation hangs) match verbatim, and "without overdoing it" maps directly to "don't overdo it." It is consistent with established climbing-training and sport-science consensus that recovery/deload periods favor reduced load and active recovery over complete cessation (Horst's framework explicitly endorses this; not contradicted by Lattice or Anderson RCTM). The only minor liberty is shifting the source's permissive "can include" to a mildly prescriptive "should use active recovery rather than total rest," but the claim is attributed as guidance, preserves the caveat, cites no implausible numbers, and does not present it as a universal law. Within faithful, plausible range.

confirmed.claim: The rationale is a fitness-minus-fatigue model: hard training accumulates fatigue and hits diminishing returns after several weeks, so scheduled deloads let fatigue dissipate and allow accumulated fitness to be expressed (and reduce injury risk).
confirmed.quote: the episode addresses how fatigue accumulates with training and why strategic deload weeks are essential for managing that fatigue and optimizing climbing fitness... diminishing returns after several weeks of hard training
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its supporting quote (fatigue accumulates, diminishing returns after several weeks, deloads manage fatigue and optimize fitness). The "fitness-minus-fatigue" framing is the standard Banister fitness-fatigue/impulse-response model underlying periodization theory; describing deloads as letting fatigue dissipate so accumulated fitness is "expressed" is a correct application (performance = fitness − fatigue, fatigue decays faster than fitness). Reduced injury risk is an uncontroversial secondary rationale consistent with Lattice/Hörst/Anderson. No implausible numbers ("several weeks" matches typical 3–6 week loading blocks), no overreach, and the model is presented as a general rationale, not one coach's protocol stated as universal law.

confirmed.claim: Lattice recommends a deload pattern of 3 weeks on / 1 week off (a 3:1 ratio) as the most common deload cadence for climbers.
confirmed.quote: A deload week involves reducing both volume and intensity to allow for supercompensation (adaptation leading to higher performance post-training), with the most common pattern being 3 weeks on 1 week off.
confirmed.evidence: The claim faithfully restates its supporting quote, which explicitly says "the most common pattern being 3 weeks on 1 week off." The claim's "3:1 ratio" is the correct expression of "3 weeks on / 1 week off," and it attributes the statement to the Lattice source rather than inventing a universal law. The 3:1 load-to-deload cadence is a standard, widely cited periodization pattern in both mainstream S&C and climbing training (consistent with Lattice/Hörst/Anderson-style programming), so it is plausible and consensus-aligned. Minor nuance: the source frames deload as cutting BOTH volume and intensity, whereas this project's ADR-0003 deliberately holds intensity and cuts only volume — but the claim is specifically about cadence/ratio, not mechanism, so this does not undermine it. No implausible numbers, no overreach beyond the quote.

genuineRefuted.claim: A full periodized macrocycle for climbing energy-system development uses an extended base phase of roughly 4 months followed by a ~2-month peak phase, allowing time for complete aerobic and anaerobic adaptation before performance.
The taper produces a peaking effect by recalibrating the brain's central governor, unlocking higher power output, increased stamina, and a new performance extreme.
